section: Improving impact toughness of Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel by optimizing grain boundaries via multi-stage heat treatment without compromising high strength and ductility

context_update.summary: This chunk is the abstract and introduction. It defines the material Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C–2Ni–3Cr (wt.%) and the three heat treatment conditions S1000, S1050, and MHT, without giving exact temperature–time schedules. Reported grain sizes are S1000: 3.17 μm, S1050: 3.84 μm, MHT: 7.31 μm, with special grain boundary fractions 71.7%, 68.3%, 71.2% respectively. MHT dissolves intergranular Cr7C3 precipitates present in S1000. MHT achieves half-size impact energy 55.1 J (88.6% higher than S1000) while keeping UTS ~1100 MPa, TE ~50%, and YS 910 MPa. The toughness increase is attributed to higher crack propagation energy from combined precipitate dissolution and high special boundary fraction. The introduction describes Al reducing density 1.5% per wt%, Ni forming unshearable B2 to raise work‑hardening, Cr inhibiting κ‑carbides, and cites Kim’s results (Fe–20Mn–12Al-1.5C achieving YS 994 MPa, UTS 1093 MPa, TE 40%). No figures or tables are referenced in this chunk. The following sections will likely detail heat treatment parameters, microstructural characterization, and quantitative property analysis.
processing_route: multi-stage heat treatment (MHT) and single-stage S1000, S1050; temperature/time values not stated here
properties: MHT half-size impact energy 55.1 J (88.6% higher than S1000)
mechanisms: combined effect of precipitate dissolution and high fraction of special grain boundaries
solid-solution, grain boundary, and precipitation strengthening
unresolved_terms: exact MHT schedule (temperatures, times, quenching media)
S1100 condition (mentioned in paper_map but not in this chunk)
prior processing details (melting, homogenization, hot rolling)

context_update.summary: This chunk covers experimental methods and initial microstructure. The steel composition (nominal Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.5C-2Ni-3Cr, analyzed Fe-20.3Mn-8.6Al-1.4C-2.1Ni-2.9Cr wt.%) and density (6.78 g/cm³, 14% lower than iron) are provided. Processing includes vacuum melting, homogenization at 1200°C/2h, hot-rolling at 1050–950°C to 6 mm, and air cooling. Four heat treatments are defined: single-stage S1000 (1000°C/3min/quench), S1050 (1050°C/3min/quench), S1100 (1100°C/3min/quench, only for tensile comparison), and multi-stage MHT (1000°C/3min → 750°C/15min → 1050°C/3min, all quenched). Microstructural characterization employs SEM, EBSD, TEM; grain size includes twins as effective grain size deff; special boundaries are Σ≤29; C content estimated via XRD lattice parameter equations. Mechanical testing details: tensile specimens (gauge 25×4×5 mm³, 3 mm/min, 0.2% offset YS), Charpy specimens (5×10×55 mm³, three tests average). Results 3.1 shows: S1000 contains grain-boundary Cr7C3 that dissolves in S1050 and MHT; deff values: 3.17 μm (S1000), 3.84 μm (S1050), 7.31 μm (MHT); special boundary fractions: 71.7%, 68.3%, 71.2%; peak misorientation at 60° (Σ3 twins); κ-carbides 3–10 nm coherent with [011]γ//[011]κ; B2 phase 20–160 nm in S1050, slightly smaller in MHT. Figures 1–3 are referenced. Subsequent chunks are expected to provide tensile/impact property data and toughening mechanisms.
material_systems: Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel (nominal); analyzed Fe-20.3Mn-8.6Al-1.4C-2.1Ni-2.9Cr wt.%
microstructure: Cr7C3 at grain boundaries in S1000
B2 particles (20–160 nm in S1050, slightly smaller in MHT)
unresolved_terms: tensile properties (YS, UTS, TE) for all conditions
impact energy and crack propagation/initiation energy
toughening mechanism linking special boundaries and precipitate dissolution

context_update.summary: This chunk provides detailed TEM characterization confirming κ-carbide (3–10 nm, orientation [011]γ//[011]κ) in all specimens, Cr7C3 on S1000 grain boundaries that dissolves at 1050°C, and B2 phase (20–160 nm in S1050, slightly smaller in MHT). Tensile tests (Fig.4, Table 1) show S1000, S1050, and MHT retain high strength–ductility, with YS/UTS decreasing mildly with grain size; S1100 yields 827 MPa/998 MPa. Strain hardening curve reaches a maximum of 2000 MPa at strain 0.20–0.22. Impact results (Fig.5, Table 1) reveal MHT impact energy 55.1 J (88.6% increase over S1000), dominated by crack propagation energy (36.78 J) and larger displacement (8.04 mm). Fractography (Figs.6–7) shows intergranular fracture in S1000 vs. ductile fracture in MHT, with shear zone area increase. These findings establish that MHT primarily enhances impact toughness through increased crack propagation resistance without severe loss in tensile properties, linked to grain boundary optimization and precipitate dissolution.
microstructure: κ-carbide 3–10 nm coherent with austenite, orientation [011]γ//[011]κ
Cr7C3 on grain boundaries in S1000 dissolved at 1050°C
B2 phase 20–160 nm in S1050, smaller in MHT
properties: S1000: grain 3.17 µm, YS 955 MPa, UTS 1126 MPa, TE 55.5%, impact 29.2 J
S1050: grain 3.84 µm, YS 945 MPa, UTS 1113 MPa, TE 55.8%, impact 42.8 J
MHT: grain 7.31 µm, YS 910 MPa, UTS 1076 MPa, TE 50.9%, impact 55.1 J
strain hardening max 2000 MPa at ε=0.20–0.22
crack propagation energies: 9.87 J (S1000), 26.64 J (S1050), 36.78 J (MHT)
mechanisms: crack propagation energy dominates impact toughness difference
intergranular fracture in S1000 due to Cr7C3
ductile fracture with dimples in MHT associated with special grain boundaries and no precipitates
quasi-cleavage and slip bands in S1000/S1050

context_update.summary: This chunk covers impact fracture morphology (Fig. 7), quantitative strengthening analysis, and detailed toughening mechanisms. The fracture morphology shows S1000 intergranular fracture, while MHT exhibits ductile dimpled fracture without quasi-cleavage. In the discussion, the yield strength is decomposed into solid-solution (σ0), grain boundary (σGR) and precipitation (σPPT) contributions using a Hall-Petch relation with K=461 MPa μm^1/2. EDS-measured austenite compositions (Table 2) and the calculated σ0 values (~600 MPa) are similar among samples. σGR decreases with increasing effective grain size from 145 MPa (S1000) to 63 MPa (MHT). σPPT values are 209, 216, and 248 MPa, showing no significant difference, indicating minor variations in tensile properties. Special boundary fractions (Σ≤29) are 71.7% (S1000), 68.3% (S1050), and 71.2% (MHT). Crack initiation energy is comparable for S1000 and MHT, while crack propagation energy increases from S1000 to MHT. Crack path analysis (Fig. 8) reveals that Cr7C3 precipitates in S1000 initiate intergranular cracks along RHAGBs; in S1050 elimination of precipitates leads to mixed fracture; in MHT larger grain size and more special boundaries cause continuous crack deflection, increasing effective propagation length and energy dissipation, boosting toughness by 29%. The 750°C intermediate step in MHT creates grain-boundary precipitates that promote twin nucleation and special boundary formation (Fig. 9). A schematic (Fig. 10) summarizes the two crack propagation modes.
properties: MHT impact toughness increase by 29% (via crack propagation discussion)
mechanisms: crack initiation vs propagation energy analysis
crack deflection and path lengthening by special boundaries and grain size
effect of precipitate dissolution on intergranular cracking suppression
intermediate temperature precipitate-induced twin nucleation for special boundary enhancement

Crack path analysis (Fig. 8) reveals that Cr7C3 precipitates in S1000 initiate intergranular cracks along RHAGBs; in S1050 elimination of precipitates leads to mixed fracture; in MHT larger grain size and more special boundaries cause continuous crack deflection, increasing effective propagation length and energy dissipation, boosting toughness by 29%. The 750°C intermediate step in MHT creates grain-boundary precipitates that promote twin nucleation and special boundary formation (Fig. 9). A schematic (Fig. 10) summarizes the two crack propagation modes.
Material systems: Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel; Fe–20Mn–12Al-1.5C (referenced by Kim); Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C–2Ni–3Cr low-density steel (nominal); analyzed Fe-20.3Mn-8.6Al-1.4C-2.1Ni-2.9Cr wt.%; κ-carbide; Cr7C3; B2; B2 phase
Sample aliases: S1000; S1050; MHT; S1100
Processing route: multi-stage heat treatment (MHT) and single-stage S1000, S1050; temperature/time values not stated here; vacuum induction melting; homogenization 1200°C 2h; hot rolling 1050–950°C to 6 mm; air cooling; S1000: 1000°C 3min quench; S1050: 1050°C 3min quench; S1100: 1100°C 3min quench (tensile only)
Key variables: grain size; special grain boundary fraction; intergranular Cr7C3 precipitates; impact energy; ultimate tensile strength; total elongation; yield strength; effective grain size deff (including twins)
Microstructure: austenite matrix; κ-carbides (inhibited by Cr); B2 phase from Ni; Cr7C3 precipitates at grain boundaries; Cr7C3 at grain boundaries in S1000; κ-carbides 3–10 nm co-gridded [011]γ//[011]κ; B2 particles (20–160 nm in S1050, slightly smaller in MHT); Σ3 annealing twins (60° misorientation peak)
Properties: MHT half-size impact energy 55.1 J (88.6% higher than S1000); UTS ~1100 MPa; TE ~50%; YS 910 MPa; S1000: grain 3.17 µm, YS 955 MPa, UTS 1126 MPa, TE 55.5%, impact 29.2 J; S1050: grain 3.84 µm, YS 945 MPa, UTS 1113 MPa, TE 55.8%, impact 42.8 J; MHT: grain 7.31 µm, YS 910 MPa, UTS 1076 MPa, TE 50.9%, impact 55.1 J; S1100: YS 827 MPa, UTS 998 MPa

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the final part of the Toughening mechanisms discussion and the full Conclusions section. It summarizes crack propagation modes in S1000 and MHT: S1000 exhibits rapid crack propagation along random high-angle boundaries due to grain-boundary Cr7C3 precipitates, while MHT shows crack deflection and hindered propagation along low-energy special boundaries, increasing crack propagation energy and toughness. The conclusions restate the microstructural parameters: S1000 (grain size 3.17 μm, 71.7% special boundaries, Cr7C3 and κ-carbides), S1050 (3.84 μm, 68.3% special boundaries, no Cr7C3), and MHT (7.31 μm, 71.2% special boundaries, no Cr7C3). MHT achieves YS 910 MPa, UTS 1076 MPa, TE 50.9%, and impact toughness 55.1 J. Strengthening is attributed to solid-solution and precipitation hardening; toughening arises from the synergy of precipitate dissolution and low-energy special boundaries, primarily enhancing crack propagation energy. Figures 9 and 10 are introduced: Fig. 9 shows SEM of intermediate 750°C-treated cross-sections, and Fig. 10 schematically compares crack propagation paths.
microstructure: S1000: austenite grain size 3.17 μm, 71.7% special boundaries, intergranular Cr7C3, κ-carbides 3–10 nm
S1050: grain size 3.84 μm, 68.3% special boundaries, Cr7C3 dissolved
MHT: grain size 7.31 μm, 71.2% special boundaries, Cr7C3 dissolved
properties: MHT: YS 910 MPa, UTS 1076 MPa, TE 50.9%, impact toughness 55.1 J
mechanisms: crack propagation along random high-angle boundaries vs. deflection along special boundaries
synergistic effect of intergranular precipitate dissolution and low-energy special boundaries enhances toughness
strength mainly from solid-solution and precipitation strengthening
